stages.name: Discovery
stages.description: Go deep on their current situation, pain points, and what an ideal outcome looks like. Your goal is to understand their world better than they do.

questions.question: Walk me through how you handle [core use case] today.
questions.purpose: Understand their current process in detail — people, tools, steps, and workarounds.
followUps: How much time does that take per week?
Who owns that process?
What breaks down most often?

questions.question: What's the biggest frustration with how things work today?
questions.purpose: Surface the emotional pain, not just the logical problem. People buy on emotion.
followUps: How long has that been the case?
What have you tried to fix it?

questions.question: If you could wave a magic wand and have this working perfectly, what does that look like?
questions.purpose: Understand the ideal future state — this becomes the basis of your pitch.
followUps: How would you measure success?
What would that be worth to your business?

questions.question: What does success look like in 12 months if you get this right?
questions.purpose: Tie the solution to business outcomes, not just features.
followUps: Who cares most about hitting that goal?
How does it affect your own performance review?

questions.question: How are your compliance and audit reporting requirements currently managed?
questions.purpose: Uncover the regulatory burden and manual effort involved in compliance workflows — a major pain point in financial services.
followUps: How much time does your team spend on audit prep each quarter?
Have you had any findings or close calls in recent audits?
Which regulations are most burdensome right now — FCA, GDPR, something else?

questions.question: Where do you lose the most time or materials in your current production workflow?
questions.purpose: Identify the highest-cost inefficiencies in their production process — downtime, waste, and rework are common entry points.
followUps: How do you currently track downtime or waste?
Which line or process causes the most unplanned stops?
Does your ERP give you visibility into that in real time, or is it always after the fact?

questions.question: How do you currently manage inventory visibility across channels?
questions.purpose: Surface fragmentation between in-store, warehouse, and online inventory — a near-universal pain in retail.
followUps: How often do stockouts or oversells happen?
Is your ecommerce platform talking to your warehouse system in real time?
How do you handle returns across channels?

talkingPoints.title: Be a consultant, not a vendor
talkingPoints.content: Your job in discovery is to listen 70% of the time. Ask one question, then be quiet. Let silences breathe — the best intel comes after a pause.
tips: Resist the urge to jump to the product
Take visible notes — it signals you care

talkingPoints.title: Quantify the pain
talkingPoints.content: Push to put numbers on problems. 'Hours per week,' 'deals lost,' 'errors per month' — quantified pain becomes the ROI justification later.
tips: Use 'How often does that happen?' and 'What does that cost you?'
Even rough estimates are useful

industryTips.financial-services: Push for compliance-related cost estimates — time spent on audit prep, cost of a regulatory finding, or FTE hours on manual reporting. These numbers are compelling in business cases.
industryTips.manufacturing: Frame around OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) and cost-per-hour of downtime. Even a rough per-hour figure makes the ROI case concrete.
industryTips.retail: Tie quantification to lost sales from stockouts, markdown losses from overstock, or fulfilment error rates — all are measurable and meaningful to retail buyers.

objections.objection: I don't want to share too much before I understand your product.
objections.response: Completely fair. Let me give you a 2-minute overview of what we do, and then I'd love to ask a few questions to see if it's even relevant for your situation. Sound good?
tips: Give a crisp overview, then return to questions
Don't let it turn into a one-way product demo

stages.name: Product Pitch
stages.description: Position the product as the answer to the specific pains uncovered in discovery. Connect features to outcomes — not the other way around.

questions.question: Does this map to what you described earlier?
questions.purpose: Check for alignment after each section of the demo — keep them engaged and catch misalignment early.
followUps: Is this closer to what you were imagining?
What's your reaction to that?

questions.question: How does this compare to what you have today?
questions.purpose: Force them to make the comparison themselves — they sell themselves.
followUps: What stands out most?
Is there anything you'd want to see work differently?

talkingPoints.title: Lead with their pain, not your features
talkingPoints.content: Open with: 'Based on what you shared, you're dealing with [pain]. Here's how we solve that.' Never lead with 'Let me show you our platform.'
tips: Reference specific things they said in discovery
One pain → one capability → one outcome

talkingPoints.title: Use proof at every step
talkingPoints.content: After each major capability, drop a customer story: '[Company like theirs] used this to achieve [specific outcome].' Keep stories to 30 seconds.
tips: Match the customer to their industry/size
Use real numbers if you can

industryTips.financial-services: Reference regulated customers (banks, insurers, wealth managers) explicitly — compliance-conscious buyers want to know peers in their regulatory environment have validated the product.
industryTips.manufacturing: Use metrics like percentage reduction in downtime or improvement in OEE — plant managers and ops directors respond to operational numbers far more than generic 'efficiency' claims.
industryTips.retail: Cite sell-through rates, stockout reduction percentages, or fulfilment accuracy improvements. Retail buyers are data-driven and will ask for proof — have it ready.

talkingPoints.title: Demo the 'aha moment' first
talkingPoints.content: Don't give a full feature tour. Find the one thing that will make them say 'we need this' and show it in the first 10 minutes.
tips: Ask your SE what each persona cares about most
Skip the login screen and setup — start at the payoff

talkingPoints.title: Compliance-ready by default
talkingPoints.content: Lead with the fact that audit trails, access controls, and regulatory reporting are built into the core product — not add-ons or professional services work. Compliance teams in financial services are used to paying for compliance as an afterthought; position this as structurally different.
tips: Name the specific regulations or standards they mentioned in discovery
Offer to share the SOC 2 report and data processing addendum proactively — it signals maturity
If they have a CISO or DPO, offer a direct technical call early

talkingPoints.title: Operational continuity
talkingPoints.content: Emphasise that deployment is designed around zero-downtime integration with existing ERP and MES systems. Plants can't afford a rip-and-replace — position the product as an intelligence layer on top of what's already there, not a replacement of it.
tips: Ask which ERP/MES they're running early in the pitch — having a pre-built connector to their system is a strong closer
Frame implementation in terms of production schedule, not IT sprints — 'You can be live before your next planned maintenance window'
OEE improvement benchmarks from similar plants land better than generic ROI claims

objections.response: I hear that — there's a lot on screen. In practice, [persona] only needs to use [2-3 things] day-to-day. Want me to show you the simplified view most teams actually use?
tips: Don't defend complexity
Redirect to the simplest workflow

objections.objection: Can you just send me a recording?
objections.response: Of course — I'll send a clip of what we just covered. Out of curiosity, is there a specific area you'd want to show someone else internally?
tips: Say yes, then use it to identify internal champions
Offer to do a separate call with the other stakeholder

stages.name: Objection Handling
stages.description: Address concerns with empathy and evidence. An objection is a question in disguise — figure out the real concern before answering.

questions.question: Help me understand — is [objection] a dealbreaker, or more of a concern you'd want addressed?
questions.purpose: Test whether the objection is fatal or just a question needing an answer.
followUps: If we could address that, is there anything else that would hold you back?
What would need to be true for this to be a no-brainer?

talkingPoints.title: The ACRA framework
talkingPoints.content: Acknowledge → Clarify → Respond → Advance. Never skip Acknowledge — it defuses defensiveness. Never skip Advance — objections without next steps stall deals.
tips: 'That's a fair point...' before every response
Always end with a question or next step

talkingPoints.title: Isolate before you answer
talkingPoints.content: Before answering any objection, ask: 'Is that the main thing holding you back, or are there other concerns?' Surface all objections before tackling any one.
tips: Answering one objection at a time reveals more
Don't chase objections before you know all of them

objections.response: I hear you on price — let me ask: too expensive compared to what? Your current solution, or the value you'd get from solving this? [Pause]. If we could show you a clear ROI in the first 90 days, would cost still be the blocker?
tips: Never discount before understanding the real objection
Anchor to value, not features

industryTips.financial-services: Emphasise audit trail and compliance ROI, not just efficiency gains — the cost of a single regulatory finding or audit remediation often dwarfs the annual contract value.
industryTips.manufacturing: Frame ROI around uptime and throughput, not headcount — plant managers think in cost-per-hour-of-downtime, not FTE savings.

objections.objection: We don't have the bandwidth to implement right now.
objections.response: That's the most common thing we hear — and it's exactly why we built our onboarding the way we did. Most teams are live in [X days] with minimal involvement from your side. Can I show you what the first 30 days actually look like?
tips: Have a specific onboarding timeline ready
Offer to involve their IT team in a separate call

objections.objection: We need to evaluate other options first.
objections.response: Totally reasonable — you should evaluate. Can I ask what you're looking for that you haven't seen yet? I want to make sure you have what you need to do a fair comparison, and I can be upfront about where we win and where others might be a better fit.
tips: Position yourself as a trusted advisor, not a closer
Ask what criteria they're using — then map those to your strengths

objections.objection: We tried something like this before and it didn't work.
objections.response: That's really helpful context — thank you for sharing that. What happened? I want to understand what went wrong so I can tell you honestly if we'd run into the same issue, or if what we do differently would have changed the outcome.
tips: Don't dismiss their past experience
Find the specific failure point — it's usually implementation, not the category

objections.objection: We need to get IT / security / legal involved.
objections.response: Absolutely — we'd expect that. We work with enterprise security teams regularly and have SOC 2 Type II, SSO, and a standard security review packet ready. Want me to connect your team directly with our solutions engineer to get that started in parallel?
tips: Have your security docs ready to share
Offer a direct connection to your SE or CSM

objections.objection: We have strict IT security and procurement requirements.
objections.response: Completely understood — and we'd be concerned if you didn't. We go through enterprise security reviews regularly with banks, insurers, and asset managers. We're SOC 2 Type II certified, support SSO and MFA, and have a full security review packet ready to go. I'd suggest we loop in your CISO and procurement team early so it runs in parallel with the commercial process — want me to set up a separate technical call with our solutions engineer this week?
tips: Have the security review packet, SOC 2 report, and data processing addendum ready to send immediately
Offer to involve your SE or security team directly — don't try to answer technical security questions yourself
Frame it as 'we've done this before with firms like yours' — name-drop peer institutions if you can

objections.objection: Our peak season is coming up — we can't take on something new right now.
objections.response: That makes complete sense — the last thing you need is a distraction heading into peak. What I'd suggest is this: we use the next few weeks to do the planning and scoping work now, so that when peak is behind you, you're not starting from scratch — you're ready to move immediately. We've actually had retailers onboard in the run-up to peak and found it paid off because the new visibility helped them manage the season better. Would it be worth a quick conversation about what a post-peak timeline could look like?
tips: Don't push for a rushed implementation — validate their concern fully
Have a post-peak timeline ready with specific milestones
Reference a retailer case study that onboarded pre-peak or used the product to navigate a peak season — social proof is powerful here

stages.name: Close & Next Steps
stages.description: Secure a clear commitment — whether that's a trial, a proposal, or a signed contract. Always leave with a specific next step, date, and owner.

questions.question: Based on everything we've covered, how are you feeling about this as a fit?
questions.purpose: Soft temperature check before proposing next steps — surfaces remaining concerns.
followUps: What would make you more confident?
Is there anyone else who should weigh in before we move forward?

questions.question: What does your process look like from here to a decision?
questions.purpose: Map the buying process — how many steps, who's involved, what could slow it down.
followUps: What typically causes deals like this to stall internally?
What can I do to make this easy for you to champion internally?

questions.question: Can we put a specific date on [next step] right now?
questions.purpose: Convert vague interest into a calendar commitment.
followUps: Who else should be on that call?
I'll send a calendar invite — does [specific time] work?

talkingPoints.title: Always leave with a mutual next step
talkingPoints.content: Before ending every call, confirm: What's the next step? Who owns it? When does it happen? A next step without a date is just a hope.
tips: Say it out loud before hanging up: 'So to confirm, [name] will do X by [date] and I'll send Y by [date]'
Put it in your follow-up email within an hour

talkingPoints.title: Create urgency without being pushy
talkingPoints.content: Urgency should come from their pain, not your quota. Reference the cost of inaction: 'Based on what you shared, every month you wait is [quantified impact].'
tips: Use their own words to describe the cost of delay
Tie urgency to an upcoming event (Q4, renewal date, new initiative)

industryTips.financial-services: Tie urgency to a regulatory deadline, upcoming audit cycle, or a recent enforcement action in their sector — these create genuine, non-pushy urgency.
industryTips.retail: Tie urgency to the retail calendar — peak season, a planned range reset, or a new channel launch. Retailers think in seasons, not quarters.

talkingPoints.title: The trial close
talkingPoints.content: Before asking for the full commitment, test: 'If the proposal comes back looking like what we discussed today, is there any reason you wouldn't move forward?' Their answer tells you exactly what's left to solve.
tips: Listen carefully — any hesitation is an objection to handle now
Don't ask this if you haven't addressed all major concerns

objections.response: Of course — what specifically would be helpful to think through? I'd rather help you work through any concerns now than have you sitting on unanswered questions. What's the main thing on your mind?
tips: Never accept 'I'll think about it' without understanding what they're thinking about
Offer a specific follow-up: 'How about we reconnect Thursday — does 2pm work?'

objections.objection: I need to talk to my boss first.
objections.response: Totally makes sense — and I want to make that conversation easy for you. What's the best way I can help you present this internally? I can put together a one-pager, join a call, or send a business case template — whatever is most useful.
tips: Make them the hero, not the supplicant
Get a date for the internal conversation and follow up after
